data.title: Western Asset Total Return Unconstrained Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: During the third quarter, volatility in both equity and Treasury markets remained relatively subdued. Risk assets performed well, with the S&P 500 Index reaching new highs and credit spreads tightening. US job growth weakened in August, with nonfarm payrolls rising by 22,000 jobs—lower than July’s upwardly revised figure of 79,000 (from 73,000). The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) lowered its policy rate by 25 bps at its September meeting, setting the target range for the federal funds rate at 4.00% to 4.25%.
  • Contributors: The portfolio’s exposure to investment grade corporate bonds contributed to performance.
  • Detractors: The portfolio’s exposure to non-U.S. developed market (DM) positions was a headwind for returns.
  • Outlook: U.S. financial conditions are largely supportive, which, alongside fiscal loosening and solid household balance sheets, points toward a positive foundation for growth. Headwinds remain, including doubts over the state of the U.S. labor market, core inflation lingering close to 3% and the potential lagged impact of tariffs. This backdrop should keep the Federal Reserve on a rate-cutting path, albeit with a cautious view of the inflation outlook.

Performance Review

  • The portfolio’s exposure to investment grade corporate bonds contributed to performance.
  • Duration positioning had a positive impact on performance as yields fell.
  • Agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) positioning had a positive impact on performance as spreads tightened.
  • The portfolio’s yield-curve positioning had a positive impact on performance as yields fell.
  • The portfolio’s non-US DM positions had a negative impact on performance as gilt yields rose.

Outlook

  • Western Asset’s outlook remains constructive, though we recognize that growth momentum is tempered by uncertainty in areas such as inflation, central bank policy, geopolitics and fiscal sustainability.
  • Fiscal vulnerabilities and persistent inflation may see the yield curve steepen further. European growth is expected to benefit from German and E.U. fiscal support with the European Central Bank judging the eurozone economy to be “in a good place.”
  • We continue to focus on disciplined, fundamental, relative value investing, seeking pockets of opportunity in fixed income while remaining mindful of technical and macroeconomic risks.

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the figures shown. Principal value and investment returns will fluctuate, and investors’ shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Performance would have been lower if fees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit franklintempleton.com.

Gross expenses are the fund’s total annual operating expenses as of the fund’s prospectus available at the time of publication. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns. Net expenses reflect contractual fee waivers, expense caps and/or reimbursements, which cannot be terminated prior to 12/31/2026 without Board consent. Additional amounts may be voluntarily waived and/or reimbursed and may be modified or discontinued at any time without notice.

Maximum sales charges have been updated as of August 15, 2022, please refer the Fund’s prospectus for more information.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Fixed income securities involve interest rate, credit, inflation and reinvestment risks, and possible loss of principal. As interest rates rise, the value of fixed income securities falls. Asset-backed, mortgage-backed or mortgage-related securities are subject to prepayment and extension risks. Low-rated, high-yield bonds are subject to greater price volatility, illiquidity and possibility of default. International investments are subject to special risks, including currency fluctuations and social, economic and political uncertainties, which could increase volatility. These risks are magnified in emerging markets. Derivative instruments can be illiquid, may disproportionately increase losses, and have a potentially large impact on performance. Leverage increases the volatility of investment returns and subjects investments to magnified losses and a decline in value. These and other risks are discussed in the fund’s prospectus.

Glossary

Duration is a measure of the sensitivity of a bond’s price to changes in interest rates.

The federal funds rate is the interest rate at which a depository institution lends funds maintained at the Federal Reserve to another depository institution overnight. Also known as the “fed funds rate”.

Investment grade is a rating that indicates that a municipal or corporate bond has a relatively low risk of default. BBB is medium credit quality rating.

The S&P 500 Index features 500 leading U.S. publicly traded companies, with a primary emphasis on market capitalization. Source: © S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC. All rights reserved. The yield curve shows the relationship between yields and maturity dates for a similar class of bonds.

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager’s assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund’s portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

The ICE BofA US Dollar 3-Month Deposit Offered Rate Constant Maturity Index tracks the performance of a synthetic asset paying Libor to a stated maturity. The index is based on the assumed purchase at par of a synthetic instrument having exactly its stated maturity and with a coupon equal to that day’s 1xing rate. That issue is assumed to be sold the following business day (priced at a yield equal to the current day fixing rate) and rolled into a new instrument. Source: The index data referenced herein is the property of Intercontinental Exchange (“ICE”) and/or its licensors and has been licensed for use by Franklin Templeton. ICE and its licensors accept no liability in connection with this use. The Bloomberg US Aggregate Index is comprised of investment-grade, U.S. dollar-denominated government, corporate, and mortgage- and asset-backed issues having at least one year to maturity. Source: Bloomberg Indices. The ICE BofA 3-Month U.S. Treasury Bill Index is an unmanaged index that comprises a single U.S. Treasury issue with approximately three months to final maturity, purchased at the beginning of each month and held for one full month. Source: The index data referenced herein is the property of Intercontinental Exchange (“ICE”) and/or its licensors and has been licensed for use by Franklin Templeton. ICE and its licensors accept no liability in connection with this use.

Important data provider notices and terms available at www.franklintempletondatasources.com. All data is subject to change.

The 30-day SEC yield is calculated using the net income (interest and dividends) per share earned over a trailing 30-day period (annualized), divided by the fund’s share price at the end of that period. It may not equal the fund’s actual income distribution rate, which reflects the fund’s past dividends paid to shareholders.

© Franklin Distributors, LLC. Member FINRA/SIPC.

Western Asset Management Company, LLC, and Franklin Distributors, LLC, are Franklin Templeton companies.

Before investing, carefully consider a fund’s investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. You can find this and other information in each prospectus, or summary prospectus, if available, at www.franklintempleton.com. Please read it carefully.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Driven Brands: Not Joining The Ride
data.description:
Shares of Driven Brands (DRVN) have surfaced on my radar recently after the business announced the sale of the remaining (European) car wash operations after selling similar US assets earlier in 2025. Designed to address elevated leverage, the company is resorting to some asset sales to keep its financial house in order after the business has been struggling since its offering in 2021.

Given all this, I am cautious as asset sales take place at not too demanding valuations. This makes me cautious to get involved here, with leverage being elevated and while earnings power looks solid, earnings are quite adjusted, and growth is difficult to find. Even as shares have been lagging, Driven does not look like a very compelling opportunity here.

Automotive Services

Driven Brands is one of the larger automotive service companies in North America. The company operates nearly 5,000 locations in which clients (both consumers and businesses) can obtain automotive services, including paint, collision, glass repair, oil change, maintenance, and car wash. The company operates brands like Maaco, Meineke, and CARSTAR.

Just like many restaurant chains, Driven operates a dual business model in the sense that it has company-owned stores yet has franchise operations as well.

Early in 2025, the company reported its 2024 results, a year in which revenues rose by about 1.5% to $2.34 billion. The vast majority of these revenues are generated from company-owned stores, at more than $1.5 billion in sales. This is complemented by franchise royalties & fees, independently operated stores, advertising, and supply chain revenues.

Looking at the actual sources of revenues, it is maintenance that makes up about $1.1 billion in very lucrative sales, with EBITDA margins reported in the mid-thirties. Many of these sales are from operated locations, with systemwide sales reported at $2.1 billion.

The second-largest reported revenue numbers came from car wash, a $587 million owned segment with segment margins seen around 20% of sales. Paint, collision, and glass make up just over $400 million in sales, a fraction of its system-wide sales, complemented by smaller platform services.

The company reported a GAAP loss of $292 million, this being largely driven by a $389 million impairment charge. Adjusted for this and other items, adjusted earnings were reported at $1.14 per share, up $0.29 from the year before. I have some real issues with that number, in part because it excludes $48 million in pre-tax stock-based compensation expenses, those equaling $0.30 per share on a pre-tax basis.

Modest Inroads

Saddled with debt, the business went public in 2021, and after trading around the $30 mark in the years after the offering, shares plunged in 2023 to trade ever since then in a $10–$20 range.

This comes as the business has seen tougher organic growth but, moreover, has real debt apparent on the balance sheet as well. In fact, net debt stood at $2.5 billion as of 2024, all while adjusted EBITDA that year was reported at just $552 million, for a leverage ratio in the mid-4s. Trying to address this leverage ratio, the company announced the sale of the US car wash operations in a $385 million deal alongside the release of the 2024 results.

Taking this into account in the outlook, the company guided for 2025 sales at a midpoint of $2.10 billion, EBITDA around $535 million, and adjusted earnings at a midpoint of $1.20 per share. The company maintained this outlook alongside the first two quarters of the year, yet hiked the midpoint of the full-year sales guidance to $2.11 billion alongside the third-quarter earnings report.

At the same time, the EBITDA guidance was reduced slightly to $530 million, while adjusted earnings were seen up to $1.25 per share. By now, net debt has come down to $2.05 billion, largely the result of the sale of the US car wash operations, as leverage ratios came down to 3.8 times, still being elevated.

Getting Rid of Washing Operations Altogether

Early in December, Driven announced its intention to sell IMO, the international car wash business. The company has reached a deal with Franchise Equity Partners to sell IMO in a EUR 406 million deal, which, based on prevailing exchange rates, works down to about $475 million in US dollar terms.

The company further provided an update, seeing pro forma 2025 sales at a midpoint of $1.86 billion and EBITDA around $450 million. This suggests that these operations generate about $250 million in sales and $80 million in EBITDA, which would suggest that just a modest 6 times EBITDA multiple has been fetched.

On paper, net debt would come down to $1.57 billion as the company sees leverage down 0.3 times, which seems to add up. Given the cheaper multiple fetched, the company actually trimmed the full-year adjusted earnings guidance by around $0.05.

What Now?

The remaining company has 165 million shares trading around $15 per share, for a $2.5 billion equity valuation and a $4.0 billion enterprise valuation. With pro forma EBITDA down to $450 million, all of Driven trades at near 9 times EBITDA here, as the deal to sell the washing operations seems quite dilutive here.

While adjusted earnings numbers around $1.20 per share worked down to a modest 12–13 times earnings multiple, this is too simplistic, and after correcting for stock-based compensation expenses, earnings trend closer to $1 per share, for a 15 times earnings multiple. This looks rich given modest organic growth, still quite some leverage apparent, and adjusted earnings numbers used here, with more adjustment appearing than simply the stock-based compensation expense.

Given all this, I am erring on the side of caution with Driven being an underperforming stock since its offering in 2021. High debt, associated costs, adjusted earnings, and now some cheaper divestment of the car wash operations make me cautious here, as the leverage situation is still not solved right now.

In between all this, I am happy to provide coverage on the business in 2026, but recent actions and the situation from the outset do not create a pretty picture, even after a period of a rather dismal performance here.

data.title: Akebia Post-Selloff: Revenue Dynamics And Upcoming Catalysts
data.description:
Thesis: Vafseo post sell-off

As you know, Akebia Therapeutics, Inc. (AKBA) makes money mainly via the commercialisation of Vafseo. It's an oral therapy approved for the treatment of anaemia due to chronic kidney disease (CKD) in adults receiving dialysis. Recently, the company has focused Vafseo purely on those receiving dialysis and has opted to discontinue efforts to get the drug approval expanded to non-dialysis patients. This was a big change for the stock; many others saw Vafseo's expansion as one of the key catalysts for the company. In my previous coverage, I tipped that we would see a pretty big inflexion in vafseo but with the market pricing out the potential of non-dialysis patients, my buy call is down about 53%.

Now, Vafseo sales in dialysis patients represent the company's core revenue-generating product. Vafseo is sold to and reimbursed mainly through large dialysis organisations and healthcare providers that manage in-centre dialysis patients. Now, this is a highly concentrated and protocol-driven customer base. It's also a hotly contested environment in which Akebia competes with injectable erythropoiesis-stimulating agents. Their drug, however, can offer a once-daily oral alternative. I think with Vafseo being an oral treatment, it screens as very attractive for those on dialysis. Akebia's revenue would be driven by patient starts, dose levels, adherence, and ongoing refill behaviour within dialysis centres. In addition to Vafseo, Akebia also derives a bit of revenue from Auryxia, which is an iron-based phosphate binder also used in patients with CKD on dialysis. Those seen with iron deficiency anaemia would be the main candidates for Auryxia. However, as of now, Vafseo is the primary strategic growth driver.

Pipeline momentum to pick up in 2026

With Akebia abandoning their efforts to expand Vafseo, you would expect the one positive would be that they now have more resources to focus on the other candidate in their pipeline. 2026 is also set to be a big year in terms of pipeline catalysts, with quite a few key milestones coming up. Their current pipeline actually extends beyond its commercial anaemia franchise into a few mid-stage and early-stage investigational programs. Now, these pipelines mainly target serious kidney diseases. At the forefront is, of course, praliciguat, which is an sGC (soluble guanylate cyclase) stimulator now being evaluated in a Phase 2 trial for focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS). This is a very rare, progressive kidney disorder, and praliciguat's key use here is to enhance blood flow and cellular signalling in kidney tissues. As you probably heard, the first patient has already been dosed, and the next milestone coming up for the candidate will be further enrollment and some early efficacy/safety readouts.

Another key program would be AKB-097, which is a tissue-targeted anti-C3d/FH fusion protein complement inhibitor. It's specifically designed to modulate the complement cascade we see in diseases like IgA nephropathy, lupus nephritis, and C3 glomerulopathy without actually inducing broad systemic inhibition. Now, this candidate is scheduled to enter a Phase 2 basket study sometime in 2H26, with initial data then expected in 2027. Additionally, AKB-9090 is an HIF-PH inhibitor intended to protect against acute kidney injury associated with cardiac surgery. It's currently advancing into Phase 1 early this year with the aim of demonstrating safety and proof-of-mechanism in this acute setting.

2026 outlook

Akebia has just given us a pretty important 2026 outlook update this week. I think it shows a company entering this year with improving momentum for Vafseo/vadadustat, and also meaningful progress in their diversified kidney disease pipeline. On the commercial side of things, Akebia has secured pretty broad prescribing access, which now covers about 275,000 U.S. dialysis patients. This figure sets Vafseo up quite nicely for deeper penetration since we're seeing large dialysis organisations implement modified dosing protocols. This trend is particularly observed in-center dosing that has already produced a notable improvement in adherence. We saw the first-refill adherence rising to 91% in 4Q25, which you think would have a positive effect on sales; however, 4Q25 net revenue for Vafseo is expected to be pretty muted at just $5 to $6 million. To put that figure in context, in 3Q25 we saw $14.3 million in Vafseo sales. The drop is because of some temporary inventory reductions and protocol transitions, which we will get to. Despite this, the underlying patient demand came in at $10.5 to $11.5 million, and the company expects revenue growth to resume in 1Q26 as access is set to expand and compliance stabilises.

Clinically, however, we're still seeing Akebia building an evidence base to support Vafseo as a potential standard of care in dialysis-dependent CKD anaemia. They now have enrollment completed in the large VOICE Phase IV outcomes trial, with topline data expected sometime in early 2027. Positive post-hoc mortality and hospitalisation analyses from INNO2VATE were presented at ASN Kidney Week 2025, and additional readouts are anticipated from the VOCAL trial in 2H26. Akebia is also leveraging Vafseo-generated revenue to advance its mid-stage rare kidney disease pipeline that could actually represent its next growth driver sometime down the line. The pipeline includes dosing the first patient in a Phase 2 FSGS trial of praliciguat, and has plans for a Phase 2 basket study in complement-mediated kidney diseases for AKB-097 in 2H26. Management also told us about the intent to initiate a Phase 1 study of AKB-9090 for acute kidney injury in the first half of this year. All in all, I think the update shows us that Akebia is on the right track, despite the stock price moving in a downward trend in the latter part of 2025. Akebia is clearly still following a dual-track strategy, with stabilising/scaling a differentiated dialysis anaemia franchise on one side of the books whilst also investing pretty aggressively in nephrology-focused pipeline assets.

The risk is a dip in 4Q25 revenue

Now, one of the talking points from the update was that Akebia expects 4Q25 net revenue to be somewhat muted, falling in the range of $5 to $6 million. Now, that is mainly because of some temporary channel inventory reductions and the operational transition to new dosing protocols with some of their key dialysis providers. So it isn't actually a dip in underlying demand. During the quarter, several centres within U.S. Renal Care are in the stages of implementing or have already implemented an in-centre observed dosing protocol for Vafseo. You see, under the new protocol, both the patient's start timing and near-term ordering behaviour are altered. So ahead of this transition, centres are slowing new patient initiations and reducing average dose levels whilst recalibrating workflows. Centres also have the job of ensuring compliance with the observed dosing model, which has led to far lower immediate shipments.

The shift also resulted in a deliberate drawdown of their existing channel inventory, which is now estimated at about $4.5 to $5.0 million, since the product already on hand was consumed rather than replenished. This directly suppressed their reported net revenue in the quarter. Most importantly, however, this inventory correction is mostly just a one-time, non-recurring effect, and the patient demand we've seen in 4Q25 is actually remaining substantially higher at an estimated $10.5 to $11.5 million. SO that tells me that end-user utilisation is still outpacing reported sales. I also think the overall protocol transition itself is rather strategically positive over the medium term. The early data from centres shows us that the dosing showed a pretty sharp improvement in first-refill adherence, from about 75% earlier in 2025 to a hefty 91% in 4Q25. So that would suggest better compliance and more consistent dosing. Thus, a more durable revenue stream once this transition period is complete.

Valuation

With a share price of roughly $1.40 and a market cap of about $403 million, Akebia is trading at a valuation that mostly reflects near-term revenue noise but also quite meaningful operating leverage if Vafseo adoption accelerates, as you would expect it to after management's most recent guidance. On a forward basis, the EV to sales ratio sits at 1.8x, which puts the stock trading at more than a 50% discount to the sector median. I think this appears reasonable, given its early commercial stage that they're at, but it also suggests a somewhat limited embedded optimism from the market. Near-term earnings multiples remain quite elevated, with a 2025E GAAP price to earnings at 36x due to depressed profits. Consensus estimates also imply a sharp inflexion in profitability, with the price to earnings compressing to ~12.7x in 2026 as revenue normalises post 4Q25. Overall, the valuation screens as mostly modest on sales relative to peers, with Akebia's equity value very sensitive to successful execution on Vafseo uptake. Akebia's stock also trades with pretty meaningful daily liquidity for a small-cap biotech. The 12-month average daily trading volume is about 2.65 million shares per day, which would indicate quite active trading interest.

As of late 2025, Akebia now sits on a pretty strong cash and cash equivalents position. They hold a sum of approximately $166 million, up a lot from the $52 million we saw at the end of 2024. There have also been successful equity raises and an improvement in operating cash flow. Now, if we look at their operating expenses, historical trends suggest a rough burn-rate of $30 to 40 million per quarter when accounting for R&D, SG&A, and clinical costs tied to their multiple mid-stage programs. So, given that level of expenditure as an estimate, Akebia's current cash sum could support operations for about 4 to 5 quarters or roughly 12 to 15 months.

Looking ahead

4Q25 is expected to come sometime around March 12th. Analysts are projecting a modest EPS improvement, with consensus estimates centred around $0.02 per share. This estimate would, of course, be driven by the ongoing revenue growth from Vafseo and cost discipline. With the drop in Vafseo sales, 4Q25 revenue is now expected to fall in the $56 million range, a figure in line with 3Q25.

data.title: 2026 EBITDA Guidance Reinforces Midstream Stability
data.description:
By Kyle Richards

Midstream is unique from the rest of energy in being able to provide EBITDA guidance for the year ahead or multi-year periods without depending on specific commodity prices. Companies provide services for fees under long-term contracts, which results in stable and predictable cash flows. Even with weaker oil prices relative to a year ago, companies are largely expecting to grow EBITDA by mid-single-digit percentages for the year. Learn more below about 2026 EBITDA guidance for companies with forecasts so far and multi-year outlooks.

Examining 2026 EBITDA Expectations So Far

Full-year financial guidance helps frame midstream’s growth profile and builds confidence in the sector’s ability to generate free cash flow to support dividend growth and opportunistic buybacks (read more). Midstream companies are generally expected to provide guidance alongside 4Q25 results, starting later this month, but some have already provided 2026 outlooks. Guidance so far indicates that companies broadly expect to continue seeing moderate EBITDA growth and ongoing free cash flow generation this year. This is particularly important with oil prices in the high $50s per barrel. Midstream tends to be somewhat insulated from weaker commodity prices by its fee-based business model.

Sunoco (SUN) stands out, with by far the most significant year-over-year growth due to acquisitions made in 2025. SUN’s projected 60+% EBITDA growth largely reflects its $9.1 billion acquisition of Parkland Corporation, which closed towards the end of last year, and the acquisition of European terminal operator TanQuid, which is expected to close this quarter.

Keyera (KEY CN) (KEYUF)(KEY:CA) is purchasing Plains All American’s (PAA/PAGP) Canadian NGL assets for $5.15 billion. However, its 7.5% EBITDA growth guidance does not yet include the purchase. KEY expects to release updated pro forma figures once the transaction closes this quarter.

Natural gas-focused names in midstream generally have stronger growth outlooks, driven by high-return investment opportunities related to the massive buildout of U.S. liquefied natural gas (LNG) export capacity (read more) and growing power demand from utilities and data centers (read more). TC Energy (TRP CN)(TRP), DT Midstream (DTM), and Kinder Morgan (KMI) are relevant examples, with KMI highlighting strong natural gas market fundamentals in its 2026 outlook (read more).

Long-Term Outlooks Point to Mid-Single-Digit Growth

While most midstream companies provide detailed year-ahead guidance, a select group also offers multi-year outlooks for adjusted EBITDA growth. For some companies, year-over-year EBITDA growth towards the end of this decade may look noticeably different from what is expected for 2026. Midstream EBITDA growth can be lumpy as new projects start up and begin generating cash flows, so companies will often reference compound annual growth rates (CAGR) in their long-term outlooks. While acknowledging that growth may not be linear, long-term targets help frame midstream’s organic growth profile over time.

In the U.S., Williams (WMB) expects a long-term adjusted EBITDA growth rate of 5-7%, and DTM also targets 5-7% adjusted EBITDA growth. HESM has guided to 5% annualized adjusted EBITDA growth through 2028 from 2026 levels. WMB is expected to refresh its long-term guidance in a strategic update alongside earnings in early February.

The Canadian midstream companies typically provide multi-year outlooks. TC Energy has guided to a CAGR of 5-7% for comparable EBITDA from 2025 to 2028. In its 3Q25 earnings update, Enbridge (ENB CN)(ENB)(ENB:CA) projected annual adjusted EBITDA growth of approximately 5% post-2026.

Keyera has guided to a 7-8% CAGR for fee-based adjusted EBITDA for 2024 to 2027 (excludes marketing), though long-term figures will be updated alongside 2026 guidance once KEY completes its purchase of PAA’s Canadian NGL assets. Pembina (PPL CN)(PBA), another Canadian midstream name that usually offers multi-year guidance, is expected to issue its long-term EBITDA growth guidance for the post-2026 timeframe this quarter. For context, Canadian names represent 28.2% of the Alerian Midstream Energy Select Index (AMEI) by weighting as of January 7.

Bottom Line

Based on 2026 guidance so far, energy infrastructure companies remain well-positioned to continue growing EBITDA by mid-single-digit percentages, supporting ongoing dividend growth and buybacks. For a deeper look into the macro landscape, dividend expectations, and more, read our full 2026 midstream outlook here.

AMEI is the underlying index for the Alerian Energy Infrastructure ETF (ENFR) and the ALPS Alerian Energy Infrastructure Portfolio (ALEFX).

Disclosure: © VettaFi LLC 2026. All rights reserved. This material has been prepared and/or issued by VettaFi LLC ("VettaFi") and/or one of its consultants or affiliates. It is provided as general information only and should not be taken as investment advice. Employees of VettaFi are prohibited from owning individual MLPs. For more information on VettaFi, visit www.vettafi.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: December CPI: Unexpected Changes Offset Each Other
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier today, the Bureau of Labor Statistics reported the Consumer Price Index, or CPI, for the month of December. The CPI is a popular measure of inflation for the economy. The report found that on a month-over-month basis, inflation rose by 0.3%. When excluding the volatility of food and energy, core inflation rose by 0.2%. On a year over year basis, overall inflation rose by 2.7%, and by 2.6% when excluding food and energy. With a few surprises deeper in the data, there’s nothing to suggest a change in monetary policy.

Goods Inflation: The Divergent Surprise

Perhaps the biggest surprise in the December CPI report was the drop in durable goods prices. Durable goods prices fell by 0.4% in the month of December, representing the largest monthly drop since 2024 and far from the inflationary effects we saw from tariffs over the summer. While year over year inflation of durable goods has never been dramatically high in 2025, it has edged down to 1.2%. The recent slide in year-over-year data may suggest the worst is behind us regarding tariffs.

While durable goods saw a great improvement, nondurable goods pricing is more mixed. Nondurable goods pricing rose by 0.8% in the month of December, the second highest level in 2025. Despite the big pop, nondurable goods inflation on a year-on-year basis is at 1.9%, which is manageable under current conditions.

Service Sector: The Source of Stickiness

Prior to the pandemic, service sector inflation was consistently between 2.5% and 3% on a year-over-year basis. Last month, year over year service sector inflation hit 3% for the first time since the pandemic inflation surge. It was a good sign that the service sector may finally begin to assist the overall inflation rate to 2%.

In December, service sector inflation rose by 0.3%. On a year-over-year basis, the inflation rate for services remained at 3%. The hold at 3% was promising as there was some skepticism regarding some of the data in the November report. Housing, with a significant presence in service sector inflation, has played a solid role in bringing disinflation to the sector.

Housing Edges Higher, Rents Edge Downward

Housing inflation did show some signs of re-firing in the month of December, rising by 0.4% for the month. This was tied for third among the highest readings over the last 12 months. After a consistent decline in year over year inflation, housing inflation on a year over year basis edged up from 3.5% to 3.6% in December. I don’t believe a single data point here is worth freaking out but should be looked at carefully in the next couple of reports.

Despite the slight setback in housing, rental inflation continued to improve. On a month over month basis, rental inflation rose by 0.3% in the month of December and 2.9% on a year over year basis. The year over year inflation of rental inflation has dropped to its lowest level (and below 3%) for the first time since October 2021.

Food Inflation: The Wildcard

While energy dominated the overall discussion regarding headline inflation (and continues to do so often), investors should keep an eye on food prices to see if a new trend is developing. While year over year food inflation remains tame at 3.1% (which is where it was in September), month over month food inflation in December came in at 0.7%, the highest level since October 2022. The next couple of reports will determine if this is an outlier or the development of a new trend.

Conclusion

While the number of unexpected moves in sector inflation rates was kind of unusual in the month of December, each move appears to have largely offset another. The drop in durable goods inflation was offset by nondurable goods and housing. With respect to monetary policy, I believe that a below-3% inflation rate may hold, and that the Federal Reserve will likely not change its plans due to this data.

data.title: SPGM And DGT: Two Global ETFs From State Street With Fundamental Differences
data.description:
2025 saw record inflows into international ETFs. As mentioned in my previous article on the iShares MSCI Intl Quality Factor ETF, Morningstar reports that the dollar weakness seen in 2025 could provide an opportunity for investors looking to diversify into global markets.

In this article, I look at two global ETFs from State Street: the SPDR® Portfolio MSCI Global Stock Market ETF (SPGM) and the State Street® SPDR® Global Dow ETF (DGT). While both provide a simple way for investors to gain global diversification, the two funds differ fundamentally.

Both SPGM and DGT performed well in 2025, with 25.27% YoY returns and 30.07% YoY returns, respectively. Both funds have outperformed the S&P 500 (SPY) by roughly 8-10 percentage points.

With that said, I aim to quantify the fundamental differences, provide a comparison of valuation and technical metrics, and make a recommendation as to which I believe is the better option for investors seeking global diversification.

Fund Overview

SPGM has an inception dating back to February 2012, while DGT has an inception dating back to September 2000, providing over 25 years of performance and fund management to consider. According to State Street’s website, the funds’ objectives are the following:

The SPDR® Portfolio MSCI Global Stock Market ETF seeks to provide investment results that, before fees and expenses, correspond generally to the total return performance of the MSCI ACWI IMI Index. - SPGM Fund Website

The State Street® SPDR® Global Dow ETF seeks to provide investment results that, before fees and expenses, correspond generally to the total return performance of the Global Dow Index. - DGT Fund Website

Additionally, State Street has been around since 1978. Managing over $5 trillion, they are a major player in the investment management space.

The Index Screening Processes

As mentioned, the two State Street Global ETFs track two separate indexes. SPGM tracks the MSCI ACWI IMI Index, a broad global equity index that includes thousands of stocks. Coverage includes large, mid, and small-cap equities from both developed and emerging markets. With such a wide breadth, the index covers approximately 99% of investable equities globally. The screening process is rules-based and market-cap weighted.

In contrast, DGT tracks the Global Dow Index. This index is much more selective. Covering 150 stocks, the index tracks a subset of global blue-chip companies. Like the MSCI ACWI IMI Index, the Global Dow Index includes both developed and emerging markets. However, unlike the other index, the Global Dow utilizes a discretionary selection process and deploys an equal weighting framework. The end result is a much more intentional selection of large, and well-known companies. While the index screening processes differ, both aim to serve as an indicator for the global market.

Holdings & Performance

Top Ten Holdings

Top ten holdings for SPGM include household names such as Nvidia (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), and Microsoft (MSFT), among others. With $1.37B in AUM and nearly 3,000 holdings, the fund’s top ten holdings make up 21.78% of the portfolio.

While the market-cap weighting for SPGM results in a greater concentration towards the top ten, DGT’s equal weighting results in much less concentration. For DGT, top holdings include vastly different companies (when compared to SPGM). Major companies include Intel (INTC), ASML Holding (ASML), and Caterpillar (CAT), among others.

Top ten holdings equal 8.66% of the fund’s total holdings.

Sector Allocation

While we can see that the underlying screening processes result in different holdings, we also observe that sector allocation differs as well, with SPGM being much more concentrated in information technology.

Specifically, as of January 8, 2026, SPGM’s sector breakdown consists of information technology (25.21%), Financials (17.72%), and industrials (12.36%). Additional sector allocations are below 11%.

Whereas SPGM is much more concentrated towards information technology, DGT is more concentrated in financials (18.27%). While information technology and industrials make up 17.03% and 13.10% of holdings, DGT proves to be the more diversified option of the two funds.

Geographical Diversification

In terms of geographical diversification, both SPGM and DGT are heavily concentrated in US equities. While this is broadly in line with global equity market weighting as a whole, DGT has slightly less US concentration (55.94% compared to 62.22%). While allocations differ between the two funds, there is quite a bit of overlap, especially in terms of European and Asian exposure.

Recent Performance

Moving on to performance, we can see on a 1-year basis that both funds outperformed the S&P 500 (SPY). While DGT slightly outperformed compared to SPGM, the difference was marginal. Additionally, if we zoom out to 3-year and 5-year periods, we see that both funds actually underperformed compared to the S&P 500 (82.39% for SPY compared to 56.16% and 70.90% for SPGM and DGT). While record inflows into international funds and demand for greater global diversification may lead to better returns for global funds in the future, it is still important for investors to consider both SPGM and DGT in terms of their entire portfolio allocation. Greater returns may be achieved elsewhere.

Valuation

On a comparative valuation basis, SPGM appears to be on the more expensive side when compared to DGT. Price/earnings, price/book, price/sales, and price/cash flow are all elevated when compared to DGT. While SPGM and DGT track different indexes, DGT proves to be the better option on a valuation metric basis for investors looking to add global diversification to their portfolios.

SPGM

DGT

Net Assets (As of 12/31/25)

$1.34B

$491.22M

Equity: Price/trailing earnings

22.29

18.77

Equity: Price/book

3.17

2.37

Equity: Price/sales

2.37

1.85

Equity: Price/cash flow

14.94

11.77

(Source: Fidelity)

Technicals

Both funds are currently in the overbought weekly RSI region, with SPGM sitting at 70.65 and DGT at 72.35, suggesting that investor optimism may soon peak. I think, given the high RSI levels, investors should wait for a pullback towards the middle-RSI range before allocating to either fund.

Additionally, both funds are currently trading above their 20-week, 50-week, and 200-week simple moving averages. While DGT tested the 200-Week SMA in October 2022, it has subsequently relied on the 50-week SMA as support. On the other hand, SPGM has consistently relied on the 200-week SMA, having tested this level in 2022, 2023, 2024, and 2025.

With the RSI currently in overbought territory and prices significantly above moving averages, investors should be cautious before entering into a position. In my opinion, I would like to see DGT return to the 50-week SMA before opening a new position.

Additional Factors & Risks

In terms of additional supporting factors, both SPGM and DGT maintain strong momentum and dividend metrics, with Seeking Alpha momentum grades of A- and A, and dividend grades of A-.

SPGM’s last dividend payment of $0.87 was on December 2, 2025, while DGT’s last dividend payment of $2.46 was on December 24, 2025. With both funds maintaining similar dividend yields (TTM) of 2.73-2.74%, the funds provide an income component for investors seeking global diversification.

As for potential negative factors, DGT’s expense ratio of 0.50% is significantly higher than that of SPGM’s of 0.09%. However, when compared to the median of all ETF’s, DGT’s expense ratio is on par.

In terms of risks, it is foreseeable that if the dollar were to recover to previous levels seen in 2024 and early 2025, inflows into international and globally diversified ETFs could slow down.

Since both funds have large allocations in US equities, both are faced with the double-edged sword of underperforming US markets and/or underperforming international markets. For investors seeking to capitalize on dollar weakness and the potential for foreign market returns exceeding domestic markets, I believe they would be better off allocating to specifically internationally focused ETFs (such as IQLT).

Conclusion

Before allocating to either SPGM or DGT, it is important to consider who these funds are designed for. Specifically, the funds provide an easy way for investors to benefit from US growth (particularly in the information technology and financial sectors), while also gaining global diversification. However, I believe better options exist if one’s thesis is based on the continuance of the US dollar decline and international markets outperforming domestic markets. With such a large allocation to US equities, both funds will likely continue to perform well if the US and global markets perform well as a whole. If we were to see a divergence between foreign and domestic markets, both funds could likely underperform.

With that said, on a comparative basis, I think DGT is the better choice for investors looking to allocate for global diversification. Given DGT’s history of outperformance, less concentration risk, and better valuation metrics, I rate DGT a “Buy” and SPGM a “Hold”. I think investors would be prudent to wait for a pullback to the 50-week SMA before opening a position in DGT.

data.title: TransMedics: Strong Growth And A Better Setup
data.description:
Investment Thesis

TransMedics (TMDX) is set to have a very strong 2026 growth year. More specifically, I believe that throughout 2026, TMDX will post accelerating revenues, thereby allowing the stock to Inflect higher.

Yes, there are several overhangs to this stock, that I will discuss with you. For instance, the business isn't generating strong free cash flows.

Nevetheless, I argue that as 2026 gets further underway, plus the business migrates towards its 2028 30% operating profit margin goals, the business will meaningfully increase its free cash flow profile.

In sum, there's a lot to be bullish about.

TransMedics' Near-Term Prospects

TransMedics helps make organ transplants happen more often. Instead of organs being rushed on ice with patchy coordination, they keep donor organs alive and functioning using their advanced technology, while also handling the logistics needed to move organs safely.

For transplant centers, the value is simple: more usable organs and fewer logistics issues. Thus, a system that works end-to-end rather than relying on a bunch of disconnected players.

What's more, as we'll soon discuss in more detail, revenue is growing strongly across all major organs, as the U.S. operation continues to scale, and their logistics network is becoming more efficient with better utilization.

Also, new heart and lung clinical programs are about to start enrolling patients, which could meaningfully boost adoption in 2026, thereby providing revenue upside.

On top of that, over in Europe, or more specifically in Italy, launches sets the stage for expanding the model internationally.

Given this positive setup, let's dive right into its fundamentals.

2026 Revenue Growth Rates Set Towards ~25% Y/Y

Having raised its guided revenues several times throughout 2025, TransMedics is likely to end the year delivering more than 36% y/y revenue growth rates.

Meanwhile, looking ahead to 2026, given the easier comparables than the prior year 2025 had versus 2024, it appears likely that TransMedics will deliver at least 25% y/y revenue growth rates.

In fact, even if TransMedics starts 2026 by guiding towards 25% y/y revenue growth rates, I strongly believe that TransMedics will follow the same playbook next year of delivering investors a steady stream of revenue raises.

Accordingly, I believe that TransMedics could actually end up delivering around 30% y/y revenue growth rates when the year is finally done and dusted.

Furthermore, to support this estimate of mine, keep in mind that Q1 2026 will set its toughest comparable quarter for 2026.

As such, after Q1 earnings are out of the way, the remainder of the year is up against much easier comparable growth rates, thereby allowing TransMedics to start reporting accelerating growth rates. This is something that investors will reward with a higher multiple and is highly bullish. We'll discuss this setup further, next.

TMDX Valuation -- 32x Forward Operating Profits

TransMedics holds about $50 million of net debt. As an Inflection investor, this is clearly a detraction from my bull case. There's no two ways around that. This weak balance sheet will continue to overhang its multiple, particularly given that the business isn't meaningfully profitable as yet. In time, this balance sheet will improve.

However, as an Inflection investor, time is always running against you, so this weak balance sheet will take time to improve, and is something for us to keep in mind, in our overall risk-reward potential expectation.

Moving on, TransMedics is on a path to end 2025 with approximately 16% operating margins. Therefore, together with further scale, there's a path for TransMedics to deliver around 21% operating margins, as the company moves towards its goal of 30% operating margins in 2028.

This means that TransMedics could see more than $155 million of operating profits, leaving the stock priced at 32x forward operating profits.

Note, typically, I'm a free cash flow investor, and I tend to skew away from businesses with high capex needs, which have high profits but lacklustre free cash flows.

However, I believe that a large amount of the capex built out TransMedics is deploying now, particularly in the US, will start to moderate in 2026 and early 2027, which will start to deliver increasing free cash flows.

Thus, even though right now I'm not able to confidently estimate its 2026 free cash flows, I am reasonably confident that its free cash flows will start to increase in 2026 and early 2027, relative to where they are now.

Risk Factors to Think About

The main risk to think about is whether or not TransMedics can in fact be able to deliver around 25% y/y top-line growth. After all, this is a growth story. And without at least 20% y/y revenue growth rates, I would be forced to reconsider my bullish case for TMDX.

Another risk to think about is that, as discussed before, TransMedics' balance sheet leaves a lot to be desired. The business is still hemorrhaging cash, and needs to frequently dilute shareholders in an effort to shore up enough cash for its operations.

For example, for the first 9 months of 2025 alone, the total share count increased by 14% y/y, and the year's reported share count hasn't even delivered. To expect that in 2026 there could be a further 15% shareholder dilution isn't unrealistic.

In simple terms, before 2026 is done and dusted, even if the share price remains unchanged, its market cap could end the year at nearly $6 billion. As a result, if 2026 turns out to be a year of more than 20% shareholder dilution, I would be forced to turn neutral on TMDX.

The Bottom Line

TransMedics is approaching an Inflection point.

Revenue growth should remain strong in 2026, with a clear path to ~25%+ y/y growth and potential upside as comps ease through the year.

At the same time, operating margins are expanding as scale improves and heavy capex begins to moderate, setting the stage for improving free cash flow.

With operating profits ramping and the stock trading at an attractive 32x forward operating earnings, the risk-reward is turning increasingly attractive and I may take a position in this name soon.

data.title: Vertiv Is A Dominant Player In AI Infrastructure Ecosystem
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Vertiv Holdings (VRT) is one of the companies that I would consider a winner in the AI revolution. The company suppliers the critical power and cooling infrastructure that keeps hyperscale data centers and AI supercomputers running 24/7. And I believe that VRT fits the GARP theme quite well. It fits that mold because it is delivering explosive growth from AI driven demand yet the market has not fully priced in its multi year runway. That is why I am rating the company a Strong Buy rating.

When I look at the Q3 Earnings result, they underscore quite a decent business with robust profitability and growth prospects. Adjusted EPS jumped 63% y/y and surpassed analyst consensus estimates. Management confidently raised its guidance across the board and now targets $10.2Bn in revenue and $4.10 [EPS]. It is also important to mention that the order momentum is red hot especially with a 1.4x book to bill ratio in Q3 which drove backlog to $9.5Bn. This obviously provides exceptional visibility into 2026 and beyond as noted by the Executive Chairman, Davide Cote:

... I am more confident that ever that we're in the early stages of a multiyear period of significant growth and value creation...

The massive backlog gives Vertiv superb revenue visibility into 2026, reinforcing the conviction that surging demand for AI ready infrastructure is just not a short term spike but a durable multi year trend. And I believe the AI driven data buildout is just the beginning and Vertiv's latest results show it is uniquely positioned to ride the secular wave for years to come.

My bullish thesis on VRT is pretty straightforward. Vertiv is rising a secular wave with a widening competitive moat. The company's focus and innovation in next gen data center tech, such as transition from liquid cooling to high voltage DC power, positions VRT to capture outsized value as AI capex booms. Meanwhile, margins and cashflows are inflecting higher, de-risking the story financially. When I look at its valuation numbers, for sure, the stock is not dirt cheap in absolute terms. But when I compare it relative to its 40% earnings growth and dominant niche, then VRT still offers an attractive GARP proposition. And just to mention, this is a case where I see strong growth runway and improving fundamentals that justify a premium valuation

Business Overview

Vertiv is a global leader in mission critical digital infrastructure for data centers. It spun out of Emersion Electric (EMR) in 2016. The company spans power management systems such as UPS, Switch gear and power distribution units. It also does thermal management, integrated racks and enclosures and modular prefabricated data center solutions. Ideally, I would say that Vertiv provides the behind the scenes hardware that keeps servers powered, cooled and operational within large scale computing facilities.

VRT is highly specialized. 80% of its revenue comes from data center customers, especially those deploying AI hardware. And this focus makes Vertiv a pure play on AI and cloud infrastructure boom unlike some of its other peers such as Eaton (ETN), ABB(ABBNY) and Schneider Electric (SBGSF). Most of the peers have split focus across industrial and electrical segments. VRT's concentrated expertise shows up in its tech leadership. For instance, the company's Liebert branded cooling system and power solutions are engineered for extreme density AI computing racks that consumer 10X more power than the traditional servers.

VRT also provides services. For instance, in August 2025, Vertiv acquired Waylay which is a gen AI software firm that will further supercharge VRT's services offering with AI based predictive analytics. This move will allow Vertiv to remotely monitor equipment health, detect anomalies and proactively prescribe maintenance actions such as minimizing downtimes for customers. Its truly a powerful move for VRT even as noted by CEO Albertazzi Giordano that its " truly one of Vertiv's superpowers."

Now that I have highlighted what the business does and its product offerings, lets have a look at the growth drivers that I believe will propel VRT higher.

Growth Drivers

First, AI and Cloud Infrastructure Boom. This is a big one. VRT's growth is being turbocharged by an extraordinary upcycle in data center spending. And this is driven largely by AI computing needs. Training advanced AI models requires massive compute power. This in turn demands robust power and cooling infrastructure. And VRT is seeing this firsthand. In Q3 alone, Americas sales jumped 43% as hyperscale and cloud customers raced to add capacity for AI workloads. Management noted that the overall data center market is accelerating and tracking at the high end of prior forecasts. Yet, industry research back this up. According to Goldman Sachs Research (GS), AI is expected to drive 165% increase in data center power demand by 2030. And this outpaces traditional IT growth by far. The proof is in the orders which I mentioned earlier on. While Vertiv's orders increased 60% y/y in Q3, other peers like Schneider Electric (SBGSF) only increased 9% while Emerson did 3%.

Vertiv's order book also provides an excellent revenue visibility looking forward. In Q3, the company ended with a $9.5backlog which is an increase of 30% from last year. Not only is the backlog large but management indicates its phasing is healthy and follows normal patterns. Also, big ticket AI projects tend to be multi phase and long duration meaning that a lot of that backlog is expected to extend into 2026 and 2027. Additionally, VRT's tight partnership with hyperscalers and tech OEMs like Nvidia (NVDA) also implies that it often wins design in on new architectures early. The CEO highlighted that VRT's upcoming 800DV power systems aligned to NVDA's 2027 roadmaps. This is definitely a sign that VRT is co-innovating with key customers on future infrastructure.

Finally, 2026 outlook from management remains bullish. While official guidance will be detailed with the full Q4 release, Vertiv has already indicated that given its unprecedented backlog and pipeline, it anticipates continued significant organic sales growth in 2025. The company is accelerating expansion plans to fulfill surging orders. This proactive stance combined with record backlog and Vertiv's comment that it has strong visibility into 2026 suggests that double-digit growth will persist into 2026. This means that all updated data points like soaring orders, record backlog, AI-fueled demand and a confident 2026 commentary serves to strengthen the investment thesis. This confirms that Vertiv is riding a powerful secular trend in AI and data center infrastructure justifying why I am rating the company a Strong Buy.

I will now go on to explain why I believe warrants a strong Buy despite trading at a premium on some of its multiples

Valuation

First, the stock trades at FWD Non-GAAP P/E of 39x and FWD EV/EBITDA of 31x. Yet, by comparison traditional electrical equipment sector average trades at 21x and 14x respectively. Also, peers lie Eaton (ETN) trade at 26x FWD Non-GAAP P/E and 21x FWD EV/EBITDA, while Schneider Electric (SBGSF) trades at 30x FWD Non-GAAP P/E and 17x FWD EV/EBITDA. Finally ABB (ABBNY) trade at 29x FWD Non-GAAP P/E and 20x FWD EV/EBITDA. So for sure, VRT is trading at a premium relative to its peers as I have demonstrated against VRT's multiples shown below.

However, Vertiv is trading almost 3x faster than those peers and the overall sector average. While VRT is expected to grow by FWD Revenue growth rate of 21%, the sector median is expected to grow at 5%. On the other side, ETN is expected to grow at 8%, while SBGSF at 9% and finally ABBNY is expected to grow at 4.14%.

Therefore, when I look at the FWD Non-GAAP PEG ratio so as to value VRT relative to both earnings and growth, I note that VRT is undervalued relative to its sector median. While Vertiv trades at 1.33x, the sector median trades at 1.95x. Again, if I compare the other peers, their FWD PEG ratios are on the higher end. SBGSF trades at 2.7x, while ABBNY trades at 2.33x and Eaton trades at 2.35x. Therefore, at the end of the day, I believe Vertiv's premium valuation is justified by its premium growth. Also, the company is trading at a 32% discount relative to the sector median.

Vertiv Key Risks

The biggest risk I see for Vertiv is hyperscaler concentration. A significant portion of VRT's revenue comes from a handful of large cloud and hyperscale customers. This customer concentration means that VRT's results could swing if even one big spender pauses or delays data center projects. Thus far, demand is robust across the board but just in case of scenario where a major AI rollout gets cut or a cloud capex cycle peaks, VRT orders could hit a speed bump.

Final Thoughts

At the end of the day, I believe Vertiv checks all the boxes of a GARP theme company. It is a dominant niche player harnessing a secular growth wave with improving profitability and prudent management. From the Q3 earnings, I concur with the executive chairman that we are indeed in the early stages of an AI driven infrastructure spending cycle. And this is one where Vertiv's solutions are indispensable. And that's why I am initiating a Strong Buy rating for Vertiv Holdings.

data.title: Germany: The Corn's About To Pop
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski, Global Head of Macro

Hopes of an early German recovery were quickly shelved

Hopes for an end to German stagnation in 2025 had to be quickly shelved. After the U-turn on fiscal stimulus and the new government coalition agreement, optimism returned to the country, only for it to enter a new phase of national depression in the second half of the year, driven by doubts about creative budget accounting and a clear lack of structural reforms. Since the end of 2022, there have been only two quarters in which the economy actually grew.

Still, this dreadful economic performance had at least one upside: at the end of the year, everyone seems to have finally understood that Germany’s economic problems are not just a fatal concatenation of unfortunate circumstances but rather the result of years of underinvestment, a lack of structural reforms and the rise of China from export destination to system rival. US tariffs and the stronger euro only aggravated the problems, but they are not the root cause.

Time to get more positive about Germany

At first glance, the past year gave little reason for optimism, but rather sent the entire country into national depression. There are good reasons to finally be more positive about the German economy. The latest macro data indicates a clear turning point in industry. Industrial orders have now increased for three consecutive months, and even the argument that the November surge was driven by bulk orders does not really concern us; with the fiscal spending programme, more of these bulk orders will come this year.

By 2026, bulk orders could be the new normal, not the exception. Which brings us to the main reason for optimism: fiscal stimulus. The announced infrastructure and defence investment plans will finally begin to reach the economy this year. Critics often overlook the sluggishness of Germany’s federal decision-making process. It took until late last year for parliament to approve the 2026 budget and almost 30 military procurement contracts. With the rapid expansion of defence production capacity, there is a good chance that a large part of the defence spending will remain within the domestic economy and not leak to other countries.

In fact, we see the defence sector as a potential positive surprise in 2026. Last but not least, even though corporates don’t get tired of complaining about elevated energy costs, the government’s decision to bring those costs down to one-third of their current levels should bring further relief, if it's implemented.

Private consumption will lag behind

While industry should stage a decent rebound, private consumption will remain muted in 2026. Over the last four years, German unemployment has increased by some 500,000 people. This gradual worsening reflects textbook economics: with the economy effectively stagnating for more than five years and industry facing severe structural challenges, a deterioration in the labour market was inevitable. This deterioration appears set to continue, as the number of corporate insolvencies has not yet declined. With ongoing uncertainty about the future of Germany’s pension system and anticipated higher costs associated with demographic change, it remains difficult to foresee a significant pickup in private consumption in the near term.

The three Rs: from rebound to recovery... only with reforms

An economic liftoff is clearly in the making, and we expect some 1% GDP growth for this year. However, the economy’s problems are deeply rooted, often structural and largely self-made, except for the China problem. Solving these issues quickly is impossible. This is a completely different challenge from some twenty years ago, when Germany had also been the ‘sick man of Europe’.

This time around, the economy almost needs a complete makeover, ranging from well-known measures such as reducing red tape and introducing e-government to mastering and lowering the financial burden of demographics or through tax cuts. It is up to German Chancellor Friedrich Merz and his government to implement these reforms this year and turn a long-awaited rebound into a sustainable recovery.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Prospect Capital Is The Last Remaining BDC Bond Opportunity With A +8% Yield
data.description:
A year ago, we started a series looking for opportunities with yields of around 8% in the world of BDCs. At the beginning, we found and described quite a few such cases, but recently they have become increasingly rare. At the moment, the only such example in the BDC universe is the OTC bond of Prospect Capital (PSEC), which currently offers a yield to maturity of over 8%. Therefore, today we will focus in more detail on this case.

PSEC Basic Information

  • Total assets (Q3 2025): around $6.6 billion
  • Current market capitalization: approximately $1.3 billion
  • Expenses:

PSEC has a total leverage of close to 53.38%. Its expense ratio is around 14%, the non-leverage expense ratio is close to 5.95%, and the gross-asset non-leverage expense ratio is around 2.69%. The 1-year z-score is a negative 0.78, and the 3-year z-score is a negative 1.62.

  • Holdings: The company has close to 92 investments across 32 industries
  • Portfolio:

85% of the portfolio is in senior and senior secured investments (71% first lien). Around 63% of the assets are unencumbered.

One of the biggest parts of the portfolio is 14% in the "Real Estate" sector, 12% in the "Health Care Providers & Services" sector, and 11% in the "Consumer Finance" sector. More information can be seen in the picture below.

  • Total returns:

The 10-year total return on NAV of PSEC is close to 4.27%, and the 5-year total return on NAV is around 1%.

  • Common stock:

The common stock is currently trading at $2.78, with a close to 57% discount.

  • Historical Performance:

And here is shown a historical 10-year premium/discount chart - you can see how the stock is traded at a significant discount most of the time:

  • Credit ratings:

PSEC has a "BB+" credit rating from S&P, cut from "BBB-" in 2024, due to the realization of the company's big losses, cutting dividends, and others. The preferred stock rating comes to "B+" from "BB". More details below:

Preferred stock

PSEC has one preferred stock at the time of writing:

(PSEC.PR.A) was issued on 07/12/2021, with a coupon rate of 5.25%. The spread to the treasury yield was 3.42%, while currently it is around 3.05%. It has close to 0.51 years to call, with a call date on 07/19/2026. Its current yield is close to 7.84%. But we are more interested in the company's bonds, so let's see them.

OTC bonds

Shown above are PSEC's over-the-counter bonds. Currently, the highest-yielding bond has a maturity date of 15/10/2028 and a yield to maturity above 8.1%. Moody's rates it "Ba1," and its yield to worst is the highest among both OTC BDC bonds and Exchange Traded BDC Debt.

Credibility

Now, we would like to provide you with a more detailed credit rating calculation based on Moody's methodology to assess the company's strengths and weaknesses.

  • Profitability: Here, we get a 3-year average Net Income/Assets of around-2%, which corresponds to a credit rating of Caa3.
  • Leverage (Asset Coverage Ratio)- Baa3: The regulatory minimum for BDCs is 150%. PSEC's asset coverage ratio cushion is close to 18%, with an asset coverage ratio of 177%:

As of September 30, 2025, our asset coverage ratio stood at 339.0% based on the outstanding principal amount of our senior securities representing indebtedness of $1,935,415 and our asset coverage ratio on our senior securities that are stock was 177.3%.

  • Asset Quality-Problem loans are close to 0.7%, which is equivalent to an A3 credit rating:

Close to 71% of assets are senior secured, which is a Baa3 rating score.

  • Debt Structure Debt distributions: Baa3.

    The secured debt is around 10% of the assets, which equates to an A2 rating.

Final Rating: The weighted average credit score corresponds to "Baa3". But when we adjust that score with a discount to NAV, the historically high IPO yields of the company's bonds, and recent problems in the underlying portfolio, we get an overall adjusted credit score of "B1". We see such a large difference between the two scores mainly because of the significant discount to the company's NAV. The market-adjusted asset coverage ratio, for example, is well below the regulatory limit. The final score is definitely below investment grade, both by the rating agencies and by our adjustments:

Conclusion

With limited yield opportunities in the BDC universe of 8%, the PSEC OTC'28 bond stands out with an attractive yield to maturity of over 8%. However, this yield reflects an average credit rating. As a result, the bond offers a higher yield opportunity for investors who are willing to accept increased risk in exchange for a better YTM.
